# Welcome

The **Health Data Literacy** series uses practical NHS and healthcare examples to explore how healthcare data should be interpreted, questioned and used within complex healthcare systems.

## The series is designed to support:

- analysts
- operational leaders
- clinicians
- managers
- decision-makers

in developing a stronger understanding of:

- healthcare metrics
- variation
- benchmarking
- inequalities
- operational performance
- population health
- analytical fairness and interpretation

## Rather than focusing purely on statistical methods, the series emphasises:

- analytical reasoning
- operational context
- fair comparison
- interpretation of healthcare metrics
- asking better questions of data

The examples throughout the series are intentionally grounded in realistic NHS operational and population health scenarios.

## Each module includes:

- practical examples
- visual explanations
- operational interpretation
- **Questions Decision-Makers Should Ask** sections to encourage critical thinking and discussion

## Guiding Philosophy

> Good healthcare analytics is not just about producing numbers.
>
> It is about helping people interpret, question and use those numbers responsibly within complex healthcare systems.

## About this Series

This series has been developed and written by **Chandan Kaur** as an independent educational resource to improve health data literacy and support better evidence-informed decision-making across healthcare.

The content draws upon more than 20 years of experience in analytics, statistics, business intelligence and healthcare, together with current national guidance and recognised evaluation methodologies where appropriate.

The series is intended to evolve over time as new modules are added and existing content is refined.


## Use of Artificial Intelligence

This series was conceived, structured and authored by **Chandan Kaur**.

OpenAI's **ChatGPT** was used as a collaborative assistant throughout the development of the series to help refine explanations, improve readability, generate diagrams and illustrations, assist with cover artwork, and support the iterative development of educational materials.

All learning objectives, educational content, NHS examples, interpretation, conclusions and editorial decisions are the responsibility of the author.

The use of artificial intelligence has been guided by the principles of **human oversight, transparency and critical review**. AI has been used to enhance communication and visual presentation, not as a substitute for subject matter expertise, professional judgement or independent thinking.

This approach reflects one of the central themes of this series: technology should support better decision-making, but responsibility and accountability remain with people.
